q: Which of the following is an example of why testing is necessary?
opts: Dynamic testing increases quality by causing test objects to fail in ways that could never be achieved by the users
Static testing is used by developers to identify failures in their code earlier than can be achieved through dynamic testing
Static analysis provides evidence to customers that the elements of the system that provide no outputs are fit for release
Reviews increase the quality of requirements specifications and lead to fewer changes being needed in derived work products
exp: a) Is not correct. If the failure can never occur with real end users, then identifying it is not especially valuable as testing is ultimately aimed at improving the work product for the end users. b) Is not correct. Static testing is used to detect defects, not failures — the use of the term 'failures' makes this incorrect. c) Is not correct. Static analysis directly detects defects in code, and this is normally information for the developer, not the customer. d) Is correct. Reviews are a form of static testing that can be applied from the very start of the SDLC and are used to find defects that can be removed before subsequent development activities waste effort on faulty requirements.

q: Which of the following statements about quality assurance (QA) and/or quality control (QC) is correct?
opts: QA is performed as part of testing
Testing is performed as part of QC
Testing is another term for QC
Testing is performed as part of QA
exp: a) Is not correct. QA concentrates on process improvement using a preventive approach, while testing is a form of QC. b) Is correct. QC aims to achieve appropriate levels of quality by identifying and correcting product defects. Testing is a significant part of QC. c) Is not correct. Although testing is a significant part of QC, other non-testing techniques are also used in QC (e.g., model checking, proof of correctness). d) Is not correct. Testing is a form of QC, not QA.

q: One of the 'principles of testing' states that exhaustive testing is impossible. Which of the following is an example of addressing this principle in practice?
opts: Creating test cases that cover every possible specified output
Documenting all possible test input variations and prioritizing these based on importance
Starting testing as early as possible with reviews and other static testing approaches
Using equivalence partitioning and boundary value analysis to generate test cases
exp: a) Is not correct. Testing every possible expected output will not address this problem — the relationship between inputs and expected results can be different for each test object. b) Is not correct. Documenting all possible input variations would itself be impractical as it would take an infinite length of time. c) Is not correct. Starting testing early addresses the 'early testing saves time and money' principle, not the exhaustive testing principle. d) Is correct. Equivalence partitioning and BVA provide a systematic way to derive a finite, representative subset of all possible test cases.

q: Which test activity involves working with test data requirements, test conditions, test environment requirements and test cases?
opts: Test design
Test execution
Test analysis
Test implementation
exp: a) Is correct. Test design involves using test conditions to create test cases and other testware such as test data requirements and test charters. Test environment requirements are also specified. b) Is not correct. Test execution involves executing test cases but does not directly cover test data requirements, environment requirements, and test conditions. c) Is not correct. Test analysis identifies and prioritizes test conditions but does not cover test data requirements, environment requirements, and test cases. d) Is not correct. Test implementation uses test cases and test data requirements to create test procedures, but does not cover test conditions.

q: Which of the following is MOST likely to impact how testing is performed for a given test object?
opts: The average level of experience of the organization's marketing team
The knowledge of users that a new system is being developed for them
The number of years' experience of the members of the test team
The end user's organizational structure for a commercial music streaming application
exp: a) Is not correct. The marketing team is unlikely to perform much testing, so their experience level is not likely to impact how testing is performed. b) Is not correct. Users' knowledge that a new system is being built for them is unlikely to affect how testing is performed. c) Is correct. The number of years' experience of the test team members determines their capabilities and knowledge (e.g., of tools and defect types) that they apply when testing. d) Is not correct. The end user's organizational structure may not even be known when testing is performed and thus has little effect on how testing is done.

q: Which of the following statements is a CORRECT example of the value of traceability?
opts: Traceability between the mitigated risks and test cases that passed provides a means of determining the level of residual risk
Traceability between user requirements and test results provides a means of measuring project progress against business goals
Traceability between testers and test cases that failed provides a means of determining the skill level of the testers
Traceability between the identified risks and written test conditions provides a means of determining which risks are worth testing
exp: a) Is not correct. To assess residual risk, traceability between ALL risks and test results is needed, not just mitigated risks and passed tests. b) Is correct. Traceability between user requirements and test results provides an indication of which user requirements have been tested, measuring project progress against business goals. c) Is not correct. Measuring tester skill by which tests passed/failed can be counter-productive. d) Is not correct. Traceability between risks and test conditions helps determine which further test conditions need to be written — determining which risks are worth testing is part of risk management.

q: Which of the following is MOST likely to be an example of a tester using a generic skill when testing?
opts: The tester's deep knowledge of a variety of computer games meant that they got on well with one of the developers who was also into gaming
The tester was a former pilot and was better able to understand the acceptance criteria for the helicopter control system
The tester previously worked as a programmer and used their skills in this area to better communicate with the business analysts
The tester was very careful not to make mistakes when they methodically generated test cases prior to starting their exploratory testing session
exp: a) Is not correct. Getting on well with one developer due to a shared hobby is not an example of a generic skill useful to testers. b) Is correct. Domain knowledge used to understand and communicate with end-users and business representatives is a generic tester skill. A former pilot's experience makes them better able to understand the acceptance criteria for a helicopter control system. c) Is not correct. Programming skills are unlikely to improve communication with business analysts. d) Is not correct. Generating test cases prior to exploratory testing contradicts one of the main tenets of exploratory testing — test cases are generated during the testing, not scripted in advance.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of the whole-team approach?
opts: It allows team members to take on any role at any time
It only needs a single team to support the complete development project
It embeds business representatives alongside developers in the same team
It generates a team synergy that benefits the entire project
exp: a) Is not correct. Team members only take on roles in which they are competent — not any role at any time. b) Is not correct. The whole-team approach applies to how a single team works; it does not suggest that only one team is needed for a complete project. c) Is not correct. The whole-team approach does not expect every team member to be involved in every important decision, including business representatives. d) Is correct. By leveraging diverse skill sets most effectively, the whole-team approach fosters superior team dynamics, promotes robust communication and collaboration, and generates a team synergy that benefits the entire project.

q: Which of the following statements about the chosen software development lifecycle is CORRECT?
opts: If agile software development is used, automation of system tests replaces the need for regression testing
If a sequential development model is used, then the dynamic testing is typically restricted to later in the lifecycle
If an iterative development model is used, then component testing is typically performed manually by developers
If an incremental development model is used, then static testing is done in early increments and dynamic testing in later increments
exp: a) Is not correct. Frequent delivery of increments necessitates extensive regression testing; automation of system tests cannot replace regression testing. b) Is correct. In a sequential model, early phases have no executable code, so static testing is performed early. Dynamic testing is only possible later when code is available. c) Is not correct. In agile/iterative development, component tests are often automated precisely because they need to run frequently. d) Is not correct. In incremental models, deliverables are produced in each increment, requiring both static and dynamic testing at all test levels for each increment.

q: Which of the following is a good testing practice that applies to all software development lifecycles?
opts: Testers should review work products as part of the next development phase
Testers should review work products as soon as drafts are available
Testers should review work products before test analysis and test design begin
Testers should review work products immediately after they are published
exp: a) Is not correct. Waiting until the next development phase means unnecessary work could start on unreviewed, flawed work products. b) Is correct. Testers should review work products as soon as drafts are available to enable early testing as part of a shift-left approach. c) Is not correct. Testers typically review work products that form the test basis as part of test analysis, not before it. d) Is not correct. Waiting until work products are published means defects that could have been found earlier will already be in the published document.

q: Which of the following is an example of a test-first approach to development?
opts: Test-Driven Development
Coverage-Driven Development
Quality-Driven Development
Feature-Driven Development
exp: a) Is correct. Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a well-known example of a test-first approach. b) Is not correct. Coverage-Driven Development is not a recognized test-first approach. c) Is not correct. Quality-Driven Development is not a recognized test-first approach. d) Is not correct. Feature-Driven Development is an agile methodology based on delivering features, not a test-first approach.

q: Which of the following statements about DevOps is CORRECT?
opts: To speed up releases, continuous integration is used to encourage developers to submit code quickly without the need to complete component testing
To be able to update and release systems on a more frequent basis, many automated regression tests are required to reduce the risk of regression
To treat both developers and operations equally, the testers will allocate more effort to release testing by operations using a shift-right approach
To create increased synergy between testers, developers and operations, the testing must become fully automated with no manual testing
exp: a) Is not correct. Developers do need to complete component testing — code must pass component tests to be admitted to the CI build. b) Is correct. DevOps provides fast feedback on code quality, automated regression testing that minimizes regression risk, and promotes a shift-left approach. c) Is not correct. Testers do not attempt to treat developers and operations equally by spending more time on release testing. d) Is not correct. Automated processes in DevOps reduce the need for manual testing, however manual testing (especially from the user's perspective) remains important.

q: Which of the following is MOST likely to be performed as part of system testing?
opts: Security testing of a credit management system by an independent test team
Testing the interface of a currency exchange system with an external banking system
Beta testing of a remote learning system by courseware developers
Testing interactions between the user interface and database of a human resources system
exp: a) Is correct. System testing examines the behavior and capabilities of the complete system and covers non-functional testing such as security testing. This type of testing is often performed by an independent test team. b) Is not correct. Testing interfaces with external systems is system integration testing. c) Is not correct. Beta testing is a type of acceptance testing performed at an external site. d) Is not correct. Testing interactions between components (UI and database) is component integration testing.

q: Which of the following statements is CORRECT?
opts: Regression tests increase in number as the project progresses, whereas the number of confirmation tests decreases as the project progresses
Regression tests are created and run when the test object is fixed, whereas confirmation tests are run whenever the test object is enhanced
Regression testing is concerned with checking that the operational environment remains unchanged, whereas confirmation testing is concerned with testing changes to the test object
Regression testing is concerned with adverse effects in unchanged code, whereas confirmation testing is concerned with testing changed code
exp: a) Is not correct. Both regression and confirmation tests typically increase in number as the project progresses. b) Is not correct. It is the other way round: confirmation tests are created and run when the test object is fixed; regression tests are run whenever the test object is enhanced. c) Is not correct. Confirmation testing verifies a defect has been fixed (testing changed code). Regression testing ensures changes do not have negative effects on unchanged software. d) Is correct. Regression testing ensures changes do not negatively affect unchanged code. Confirmation testing verifies that a defect has been fixed (changed code).

q: Which of the following is an example of a defect that can be found by static testing but NOT by dynamic testing?
opts: Lack of usability provided through the user interface
Code with no path that reaches it
Poor response times for most of the expected users
Required features that are not implemented in the code
exp: a) Is not correct. A lack of usability can be detected through either a review checklist (static) or by having users dynamically test the UI. b) Is correct. A code review can detect unreachable code. Dynamic tests can only exercise reachable code — determining unreachable code dynamically would require running every possible combination of inputs, which is impractical. c) Is not correct. Poor response times are difficult to determine without executing the code, so dynamic testing is more suitable. d) Is not correct. Both a code review (static) and dynamic testing can detect features not implemented in the code.

q: Which of the following is a benefit of early and frequent stakeholder feedback?
opts: Managers are aware of which developers are less productive
It allows project managers to prioritize their stakeholder interactions
It facilitates early communication of potential quality issues
End users better understand why the delivery of the work product is delayed
exp: a) Is not correct. Stakeholder feedback comes from business representatives and end users, not from developers. b) Is not correct. Stakeholder feedback is not used by project managers to prioritize their stakeholder interactions. c) Is correct. Obtaining feedback early and often facilitates early communication of potential quality issues, prevents requirements misunderstandings, and ensures changes are understood sooner. d) Is not correct. The feedback is from stakeholders (not to them). Early feedback should prevent delays, not explain them.

q: Match task descriptions (1-4) to review activities (A-D): 1. Quality characteristics to be evaluated and exit criteria are selected  2. Everyone has access to the work product  3. Anomalies are identified in the work product  4. Anomalies are discussed  /  A. Individual review  B. Review initiation  C. Planning  D. Communication and analysis
exp: 1. Selecting quality characteristics and exit criteria is part of Planning (C). 2. Ensuring everyone has access to the work product is part of Review initiation (B). 3. Identifying anomalies in the work product is part of Individual review (A). 4. Discussing anomalies (analyzing, determining status, ownership, required actions) is part of Communication and analysis (D). Therefore: 1C, 2B, 3A, 4D.

q: Match review roles (1-4) to responsibilities (A-D): 1. Scribe  2. Review leader  3. Facilitator  4. Manager  /  A. Ensures effective running of review meetings and sets up a safe review environment  B. Records review information such as decisions and new anomalies  C. Decides what is to be reviewed and provides resources  D. Takes overall responsibility for the review such as organizing when and where it takes place
exp: 1. Scribe (Recorder) — records review information, decisions, and anomalies (B). 2. Review Leader — takes overall responsibility, organizes when and where the review takes place (D). 3. Facilitator (Moderator) — ensures effective running of review meetings, creates a safe environment (A). 4. Manager — decides what is to be reviewed and provides resources such as staff and time (C). Therefore: 1B, 2D, 3A, 4C.

q: Which of the following statements BEST describes the difference between decision table testing and branch testing?
opts: In decision table testing, the test cases are derived from the decision statements in the code. In branch testing, the test cases are derived from knowledge of the control flow of the test object.

In decision table testing, the test cases are derived from the specification that describes the business logic. In branch testing the test cases are based on anticipation of potential defects in the source code.

In decision table testing, the test cases are derived from knowledge of the control flow of the test object. In branch testing, test cases are derived from the specification that describes the business logic.
In decision table testing, the test cases are independent of how the software is implemented. In branch testing, test cases can be created only after the design or implementation of the code.
exp: a) Is not correct. Decision table testing is a black-box technique — test cases are NOT based on decision statements in the source code. b) Is not correct. Anticipation of potential defects is used in error guessing, not branch testing. c) Is not correct. The descriptions of the two techniques are swapped. d) Is correct. Decision table testing is a black-box technique, so test cases are independent of how the software is implemented. Branch testing is a white-box technique, so test cases can only be created after the design or implementation of the code.

q: Customers of TestWash car wash have cards recording number of washes. For every 10th wash: 10% discount. For every 20th wash: 50% discount total. Which set of input data (current wash numbers) achieves the highest equivalence partition coverage?
exp: The three valid equivalence partitions are: 'no discount', '10% discount' (every 10th wash), and '50% discount' (every 20th wash). a) Is correct. 19 covers 'no discount', 20 covers '50% discount', 30 covers '10% discount' — all three partitions covered. b) Is not correct. 11 and 12 both cover 'no discount', 20 covers '50% discount' — only two partitions covered. c) Is not correct. 1 covers 'no discount', 10 and 50 both cover '10% discount' — only two partitions covered. d) Is not correct. 29 and 31 cover 'no discount', 10 and 30 cover '10% discount' — '50% discount' partition not covered.

q: A form accepts a password with length between 6 and 12 characters inclusive; otherwise it is rejected. You already have 100% 2-value BVA coverage. Which additional password lengths should be tested to achieve 100% 3-value BVA coverage?
exp: The three equivalence partitions are: too short {0-5}, OK {6-12}, too long {13+}. For full 3-value BVA, we need to test: 0, 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 11, 12, 13, 14. The 2-value BVA already covers: 0, 5, 6, 12, 13 (boundaries of each partition). The additional values needed to complete 3-value BVA are: 1, 4, 7, 11, and 14 — which are the neighbors of each boundary value.

q: A decision table has 5 rules for atherosclerosis risk (very low, low, medium, high, very high) based on cholesterol and blood pressure. TC1: Chol=125, BP=141. TC2: Chol=200, BP=201. TC3: Chol=124, BP=201. TC4: Chol=109, BP=200. TC5: Chol=201, BP=140. What is the decision table coverage?
exp: There are 5 rules (columns) in the decision table. TC1 (Chol=125, BP=141) covers Rule 4 (Chol 125-200, BP>140 → high). TC2 (Chol=200, BP=201) also covers Rule 4. TC3 (Chol=124, BP=201) covers Rule 2 (Chol≤124, BP>140 → low). TC4 (Chol=109, BP=200) also covers Rule 2. TC5 (Chol=201, BP=140) covers Rule 5 (Chol≥201 → very high). So 3 out of 5 rules are covered = (3/5)*100% = 60%.

q: A storage system can store up to three elements (variable N = current stored elements). State transition diagram has 5 valid transitions (E1-E5). Which sequence of events achieves the highest level of valid transitions coverage?
opts: Add, Remove, Add, Add, Add
Add, Add, Add, Add, Remove, Remove
Add, Add, Add, Remove, Remove
Add, Add, Add, Remove, Add
exp: a) Can be written as E1, E3, E3, E2, E4 — covers 4 out of 5 transitions (80%). b) Is infeasible — after the first three Add actions the system is FULL and there is no valid Add transition from FULL state. c) Is correct. Can be written as E1, E2, E4, E5, E3 — covers all 5 out of 5 transitions (100%). d) Can be written as E1, E2, E4, E5, E4 — covers 4 out of 5 transitions (80%).

q: You run two test cases T1 and T2 on the same code. T1 achieved 40% statement coverage and T2 achieved 65% statement coverage. Which of the following must be necessarily true?
opts: The test suite composed of T1 and T2 achieves 105% statement coverage
There exists at least one statement that must have been executed by both T1 and T2
At least 5% of the statements in the code that was tested are non-executable
The test suite composed of T1 and T2 achieves full branch coverage
exp: a) Is not correct. Coverage is always a percentage and cannot exceed 100%. b) Is correct. If the statements executed by T1 and T2 were completely disjoint, the combined coverage would be 40%+65%=105%, which is impossible. Therefore at least 5% of executable statements must have been executed by both T1 and T2. c) Is not correct. Statement coverage tells us nothing about non-executable statements. d) Is not correct. Full statement coverage does not imply full branch coverage.

q: Let the branch coverage metric be defined as BCov = (X / Y) * 100%. What do X and Y represent in this formula?
opts: X = number of decision outcomes exercised; Y = total number of decision outcomes in the code
X = number of conditional branches exercised; Y = total number of branches in the code
X = number of branches exercised by the test cases; Y = total number of branches in the code
X = number of conditional branches exercised; Y = total number of decision outcomes in the code
exp: Branch coverage includes both conditional branches (decision outcomes) AND unconditional branches (straight-line code transfers). a) Is not correct. X counts all branches exercised, not just decision outcomes. b) Is not correct. X should count all branches (conditional and unconditional), not just conditional ones. c) Is correct. Branch coverage = (number of all branches exercised / total number of all branches) * 100%. d) Is not correct. Both X and Y would only count conditional branches, ignoring unconditional branches.

q: Which TWO of the following statements provide the BEST rationale for using exploratory testing?
opts: Testers have not been allocated enough time for test design and test execution
The existing test strategy requires that testers use formal, black-box test techniques
The specification is written in a formal language that can be processed by a tool
Testers are the members of an agile team and have good programming skills
Testers are experienced in the business domain and have good analytical skills
exp: Exploratory testing is useful when there are few/inadequate specifications or significant time pressure, and is more effective when testers are experienced, have domain knowledge, and strong analytical skills. a) Is correct. Exploratory testing is useful when there is significant time pressure. b) Is not correct. Exploratory testing is not a black-box test technique. c) Is not correct. Exploratory testing is useful when specifications are poorly written, not formally specified. d) Is not correct. Programming skills are not relevant to exploratory testing. e) Is correct. Exploratory testing is more effective when testers have domain knowledge and analytical skills.

q: Which of the following BEST fits as an element of the checklist used in checklist-based testing?
opts: "The developer made an error when implementing the code"
"The achieved statement coverage exceeds 85%"
"The program works correctly regarding functional and non-functional requirements"
"The error messages are written in language that the user can understand"
exp: a) Is not correct. Checklists should contain test conditions to be verified — this is an error description, not a test condition, and is too general. b) Is not correct. Checklists should not contain items better suited as exit criteria. c) Is not correct. This is too general — it practically describes a test objective rather than a specific test condition. d) Is correct. This is a specific, verifiable test condition that can be checked by a human during testing.

q: Acceptance criteria for a user story: 'Given that the user is logged in and on the homepage, When the user clicks on the Add Item button, Then the Create Item form should appear, And the user should be able to input a name and price.' In what format is this acceptance criteria written?
opts: Rule-oriented
Scenario-oriented
Product-oriented
Process-oriented
exp: a) Is not correct. Rule-oriented format includes bullet point verification lists or tabulated input-output mappings. b) Is correct. This is the Given/When/Then format, which is scenario-oriented because it describes a scenario to be verified. c) Is not correct. There is no 'product-oriented' format for acceptance criteria. d) Is not correct. There is no 'process-oriented' format for acceptance criteria.

q: User story: 'As a registered customer, I want to view my previous orders on the company's website, so that I can keep track of my purchases.' Which of the following test cases will NOT be relevant for this user story?
opts: Customer logs in and clicks 'see order history' → system shows list of previous orders with date, order number, and total cost
Customer clicks on an order → system displays individual items, prices and quantities
Customer clicks 'Sort ascending' on order history → system shows orders sorted by order number ascending
Unregistered customer registers with valid email → system accepts registration and creates account
exp: a) Is not correct. This test case is directly related to viewing the order history. b) Is not correct. This test case is related to viewing details of a previous order. c) Is not correct. This test case is related to viewing previous orders in the order history. d) Is correct. This test case is about the registration process, which is NOT discussed in the user story. The user story is specifically about viewing previous orders, not about registration.

q: Your team follows the DevOps delivery pipeline. Steps: (1) Code development, (2) Submit code into version control and merge into 'test' branch, (3) Perform component testing. Which is BEST suited as the entry criterion for step (2)?
opts: Static analysis returns no high severity warnings for the submitted code
System version control reports no conflicts when merging code into the 'test' branch
Component tests are compiled and ready to be executed
Statement coverage is at least 80%
exp: a) Is correct. Static analysis can (and should) be checked before the code is submitted to version control — this is a valid entry criterion for step (2). b) Is not correct. This can only be checked after step (2) is performed — merge conflict reporting occurs after code is submitted and merged. c) Is not correct. This fits better as the entry criterion for step (3). d) Is not correct. This fits better as the exit criterion for step (3).

q: Estimate test effort using ratios from historical data: P1 (Dev=$800k, Test=$40k), P2 (Dev=$1.2M, Test=$130k), P3 (Dev=$600k, Test=$70k), P4 (Dev=$1M, Test=$120k). New project development effort = $800,000. What is the estimated test effort?
exp: Average development effort = ($800k + $1,200k + $600k + $1,000k) / 4 = $3,600k / 4 = $900,000. Average test effort = ($40k + $130k + $70k + $120k) / 4 = $360k / 4 = $90,000. Test-to-development ratio = $90,000 / $900,000 = 10%. Estimated test effort = 10% × $800,000 = $80,000.

q: Test cases: TC1 SEARCH product A (priority 4), TC2 SEARCH product B (priority 4), TC3 VIEW product A (priority 3), TC4 VIEW product B (priority 2), TC5 ADD product A (priority 3), TC6 ADD product B (priority 1), TC7 ORDER (priority 5). Dependencies: SEARCH→VIEW→ADD→ORDER. Which test case should be executed as the FOURTH one?
opts: TC3
TC1
TC7
TC2
exp: Dependencies require SEARCH→VIEW→ADD before ORDER for each product. TC6 (ADD product B) has priority 1, so product B should be processed first. Execution order: TC2 (SEARCH B, needed for B flow) → TC4 (VIEW B) → TC6 (ADD B) → TC1 (SEARCH A, needed for A flow) → TC3 (VIEW A) → TC5 (ADD A) → TC7 (ORDER). Therefore the fourth test is TC1.

q: According to the testing quadrants model, which of the following falls into quadrant Q1 ('technology facing' and 'support the team')?
opts: Usability testing
Functional testing
User acceptance testing
Component integration testing
exp: a) Is not correct. Usability testing is business-facing testing that critiques the product (Q3). b) Is not correct. Functional testing is business-facing testing that supports the team (Q2). c) Is not correct. User acceptance testing is business-facing testing that critiques the product (Q3). d) Is correct. Component integration testing is technology-facing testing that supports the team/guides development (Q1).

q: Match risks (1-4) to mitigation activities (A-D): 1. Ineffective loop implementation causes long responses  2. Consumers change their preferences  3. Flooding of the server room  4. Patients above certain age receive inaccurate reports  /  A. Risk acceptance  B. Testing for performance efficiency  C. Using BVA as test technique  D. Risk transfer
exp: 1. Long system responses due to loop issues → test for performance efficiency (B). 2. Consumer preference changes are out of our control → risk acceptance (A). 3. Flooding of server room causes significant loss → transfer the risk (e.g., buy insurance) (D). 4. Inaccurate reports for patients above a certain age → suggests a boundary problem → use BVA (C). Therefore: 1B, 2A, 3D, 4C.

q: Which of the following is a product quality metric?
opts: Mean time to failure
Number of defects found
Requirements coverage
Defect detection percentage
exp: a) Is correct. Mean time to failure measures maturity (a quality characteristic), making it a product quality metric. b) Is not correct. Number of defects found is a defect metric, not a product quality metric. c) Is not correct. Requirements coverage is a coverage metric, not a product quality metric. d) Is not correct. Defect detection percentage is a defect metric, not a product quality metric.

q: You are a member of a test team in North America, developing a product for a client in Europe. The team is agile and follows DevOps with CI/CD. Which of the following is the LEAST effective way to communicate test progress to the customer?
opts: Face-to-face
Dashboards
Email
Video conferencing
exp: a) Is correct (LEAST effective). The client is in a different location and time zone, making face-to-face communication very difficult or impossible. b) Is not correct. Dashboards are available to any user at any time, so time zone differences are not a hindrance. c) Is not correct. Although time difference may cause some inconvenience, email communication is not as hindered as face-to-face. d) Is not correct. Video conferencing is a convenient means; while one party may need to connect at unusual hours, it is still more practical than face-to-face.

q: Which of the following BEST describes an example of how configuration management (CM) supports testing?
opts: Having the version number of the environment, the CM tool can retrieve the version numbers of libraries, stubs and drivers used in that environment
Having a record of the values of the inputs, the CM tool can execute the test cases for these configurations and calculate the coverage
Having data about the date of purchase of a software license, the CM tool automatically generates information about the fact that the product license is coming to an end
Having the version number of the test case, the CM tool can automatically generate test data for this test case
exp: a) Is correct. For a complex configuration item (e.g., a test environment), CM records the items it consists of, their relationships, and versions — allowing retrieval of version numbers of libraries, stubs, and drivers. b) Is not correct. CM tools do not execute test cases and do not calculate coverage. c) Is not correct. A CM tool is not a license management tool. d) Is not correct. CM tools do not generate test data.

q: A sort function test log shows: TC3 (input: 8 7 3 7 1, output: 1 3 7 8, FAILED), TC4 (input: -2 -2 -2 -3 -3, output: -3 -2, FAILED), TC5 (input: 0 -2 0 3 4 4, output: -2 0 3 4, FAILED). Which provides the BEST description for a defect report?
opts: The system fails to sort several sets of numbers. Reference: TC3, TC4, TC5.
The system seems to disregard duplicates while sorting. Reference: TC3, TC4, TC5.
The system fails to sort negative numbers. Reference: TC4, TC5.
TC3, TC4 and TC5 have defects (duplicate input data) and should be corrected.
exp: a) Is not correct. While true, it does not provide much useful value for the developer to locate the defect. b) Is correct. From the test results, the system ignores duplicates and sorts the list disregarding repetitions. TC3 output is missing a 7, TC4 output is missing duplicate -2s, TC5 output is missing duplicate 0 and 4. This root cause description helps the developer find and fix the defect efficiently. c) Is not correct. The system does not fail on negative numbers per se — the problem is disregarding duplicates. d) Is not correct. The test cases themselves do not have defects — it is the system under test that has a defect.

q: Match descriptions (1-4) to test tool categories (A-D): 1. Support workflow tracking  2. Facilitate communication  3. Virtual machines  4. Support reviews  /  A. Static testing tools  B. Tools supporting scalability and deployment standardization  C. DevOps tools  D. Collaboration tools
exp: 1. Workflow tracking → DevOps tools support the DevOps delivery pipeline and workflow tracking (C). 2. Facilitate communication → Collaboration tools (D). 3. Virtual machines → Tools supporting scalability and deployment standardization such as virtual machines and containerization tools (B). 4. Support reviews → Static testing tools support reviews and static analysis (A). Therefore: 1C, 2D, 3B, 4A.

q: Which of the following is MOST likely to be a benefit of test automation?
opts: It provides coverage measures that are too complicated for humans to derive
It shares responsibility for the testing with the tool vendor
It removes the need for critical thinking when analyzing test results
It generates test cases from an analysis of the program code
exp: a) Is correct. Test automation can provide white-box coverage measures (e.g., branch, path coverage) that are too complicated for humans to derive manually for all but the most trivial code. b) Is not correct. By using test tools, responsibility for testing is NOT shared with the tool vendor — the tester remains responsible. c) Is not correct. Testers still need to apply critical thinking when analyzing anomalies in test results to determine their likely cause. d) Is not correct. Neither testers nor tools can generate complete test cases from code analysis alone, as the code provides no information on expected results.

q: Which of the following is a typical test objective?
opts: Validating that documented requirements are met
Causing failures and identifying defects
Initiating errors and identifying root causes
Verifying the test object meets user expectations
exp: a) Is not correct. Validating that documented requirements are met is incorrect — validation is concerned with meeting user requirements/expectations, while verification is concerned with meeting specified (documented) requirements. This would be correct if 'validating' were replaced with 'verifying'. b) Is correct. Causing failures and identifying defects is probably the most common objective of dynamic testing. c) Is not correct. Testers do not initiate errors (errors are made by developers and cannot really be initiated). Identifying root causes is part of debugging, not testing. d) Is not correct. Verifying the test object meets user expectations is incorrect — verification checks specified requirements, while validation checks user expectations. This would be correct if 'verifying' were replaced with 'validating'.

q: Which of the following statements BEST describes the difference between testing and debugging?
opts: Testing causes failures while debugging fixes failures
Testing is a negative activity while debugging is a positive activity
Testing determines that defects exist while debugging removes defects
Testing finds the cause of defects while debugging fixes the cause of defects
exp: a) Is not correct. Dynamic testing does cause failures, however debugging is concerned with locating and fixing defects — not fixing failures. b) Is not correct. Both testing and debugging contribute to improving quality and should be considered positively. c) Is correct. Testing determines that defects exist (directly through static testing, or indirectly by causing failures in dynamic testing). Debugging is a separate activity (normally performed by developers) concerned with locating and fixing defects. d) Is not correct. Testing finds defects (not the causes), and debugging fixes defects (not their root causes, which are typically human errors).

q: The 'absence-of-defects fallacy' is one of the principles of testing. Which of the following is an example of addressing this principle in practice?
opts: Explaining that it is not possible for testing to show the absence of defects
Supporting the end users to perform acceptance testing
Ensuring that no implementation defects remain in the delivered system
Modifying tests that cause no failures to ensure few defects remain
exp: The 'absence-of-defects fallacy' states that verifying the absence of implementation defects does not guarantee user satisfaction. To address it, the system must also be validated against users' needs and expectations. a) Is not correct. This addresses the 'testing shows the presence, not the absence of defects' principle, not the absence-of-defects fallacy. b) Is correct. Supporting end users to perform acceptance testing validates that the system meets users' needs and expectations. c) Is not correct. It is not possible to ensure no implementation defects remain, and even if it were, this still wouldn't guarantee user satisfaction. d) Is not correct. Modifying tests that cause no failures addresses the 'tests wear out' principle.

q: Which of the following test activities are MOST likely to involve the application of boundary value analysis and equivalence partitioning?
opts: Test implementation
Test design
Test execution
Test monitoring
Test analysis
exp: Test techniques are used during test analysis (to identify test conditions) and test design (to create test cases from conditions/coverage items). a) Is not correct. Test implementation assembles test cases into procedures — it does not use test techniques to create test cases. b) Is correct. Test design uses techniques like BVA and EP to create test cases. c) Is not correct. Test execution runs test procedures — test techniques are not applied here. d) Is not correct. Test monitoring checks progress against the plan — test techniques are not applied here. e) Is correct. Test analysis uses techniques to systematically identify test conditions as coverage items.

q: Match testware (1-4) to the test activities that produce them (A-D): 1. Coverage items  2. Change requests  3. Test execution schedule  4. Prioritized test conditions  /  A. Test analysis  B. Test design  C. Test implementation  D. Test completion
exp: 1. Coverage items → Test design (B) produces coverage items, prioritized test cases, test charters, test data requirements, and test environment requirements. 2. Change requests → Test completion (D) produces test completion reports, documented lessons learned, action items for improvement, and change requests (as product backlog items). 3. Test execution schedule → Test implementation (C) produces test procedures, automated test scripts, test suites, test data, and the test execution schedule. 4. Prioritized test conditions → Test analysis (A) produces prioritized test conditions and defect reports for defects identified in the test basis. Therefore: 1B, 2D, 3C, 4A.

q: Which of the following statements about the different testing roles is MOST likely to be CORRECT?
opts: In Agile software development, the test management role is the primary responsibility of the team, while the testing role is primarily the responsibility of a single individual from outside the team
The testing role is primarily responsible for test monitoring and test control, while the test management role is primarily responsible for test planning and test completion
In Agile software development, test management activities that span multiple teams are handled by a test manager outside the team, while some test management tasks are handled by the team itself
The test management role is primarily responsible for test analysis and test design, while the testing role is primarily responsible for test implementation and test execution
exp: a) Is not correct. In Agile, some test management tasks may be handled by the team itself, and testing is more likely performed by various team members following the whole-team approach — not a single individual from outside. b) Is not correct. The test management role primarily involves test planning, test monitoring, test control, and test completion. It is wrong to say the testing role is primarily responsible for test monitoring and test control. c) Is correct. In Agile, some test management tasks are handled by the team. For activities spanning multiple teams, test managers outside the team may perform those tasks. d) Is not correct. The test management role involves test planning, monitoring, control, and completion. The testing role is responsible for the technical aspects: test analysis, test design, test implementation, and test execution.

q: Which of the following is an advantage of the whole-team approach?
opts: Teams with no testers
Improved team dynamics
Specialist team members
Larger team sizes
exp: a) Is not correct. Testers play a vital role in the whole-team approach, sharing their testing expertise and guiding product development. b) Is correct. By leveraging the diverse skill sets of each team member most effectively, the whole-team approach fosters superior team dynamics, promotes robust communication and collaboration, and generates a synergistic effect that benefits the entire project. c) Is not correct. The whole-team approach allows any team member with the requisite skills to undertake any task — specialist team members are not a specific advantage of this approach. d) Is not correct. There is no guidance suggesting that larger teams are an advantage of the whole-team approach.

q: Which of the following statements about the independence of testing is CORRECT?
opts: Independent testers will find defects due to their different technical perspective from developers, but their independence may lead to an adversarial relationship with the developers
Developers' familiarity with their own code means they only find a few defects in it, however their shared software background with testers means these defects would also be found by the testers
Independent testing requires testers who are outside the developer's team and ideally from outside the organization, however these testers find it difficult to understand the application domain
Testers from outside the developer's team are more independent than testers from within the team, but the testers from within the team are more likely to be blamed for delays in product release
exp: a) Is correct. The primary benefit of independence of testing is that testers identify different types of failures due to their varied backgrounds, technical viewpoints, and biases. However, the main disadvantage is that testers may become isolated from the development team, leading to communication problems and potentially an adversarial relationship. b) Is not correct. Developers' familiarity with the code actually helps them find many defects. Also, developers and testers finding different defects is due to their different (not shared) backgrounds. c) Is not correct. Testing can be performed at multiple levels of independence — testers do not need to come from outside the organization. Domain knowledge varies independently of the level of independence. d) Is not correct. Testers from outside the team are generally more isolated and thus more likely (not less likely) to be blamed for delays.

q: Which of the following is a good testing practice that applies to all software development lifecycles?
opts: For each test level, there is a corresponding development level
For each test objective, there is a corresponding development objective
For every test activity, there is a corresponding user activity
For every development activity, there is a corresponding test activity
exp: a) Is not correct. The term 'development level' has no common understanding and does not map cleanly to test levels. b) Is not correct. Test objectives (e.g., ensuring contractual compliance with required testing) do not necessarily have corresponding development objectives. c) Is not correct. Some test activities focus on developers (e.g., testing for maintainability) and have no user aspect; also, some systems have no easily identifiable end users. d) Is correct. Quality control applies to all development activities, meaning that every software development activity has a corresponding test activity.

q: Which of the following is an example of a test-first approach to development?
opts: Component Test-Driven Development
Integration Test-Driven Development
System Test-Driven Development
Acceptance Test-Driven Development
exp: a) Is not correct. Component Test-Driven Development is not a recognized test-first approach. b) Is not correct. Integration Test-Driven Development is not a recognized test-first approach. c) Is not correct. System Test-Driven Development is not a recognized test-first approach. d) Is correct. Acceptance Test-Driven Development (ATDD) is a well-known example of a test-first approach to development.

q: Which of the following provides the BEST description of shift-left?
opts: When agreed by the developers, manual activities on the left-hand side of the test process are automated to support the principle of 'early testing saves time and money'
Where cost-effective, test activities are moved earlier in the software development lifecycle (SDLC) to reduce the total cost of quality by reducing the number of defects found later in the SDLC
When they have spare time available, testers are required to automate tests for regression testing, starting with component tests and component integration tests
When available, testers are trained to perform tasks early in the SDLC to allow more test activities to be automated later in the SDLC
exp: a) Is not correct. There is no 'left-hand side of the test process'. Shift-left means moving test activities earlier in the SDLC (portrayed as moving left on a timeline). b) Is correct. Shift-left emphasizes starting testing earlier in the SDLC. This requires additional training and increased early effort and costs, but overall savings should be higher. c) Is not correct. Automating regression tests (component and integration) is generally handled by developers, not testers with spare time, and is not part of shift-left. d) Is not correct. Training testers to work earlier supports shift-left, but automating more test activities to be performed later is not part of shift-left.

q: Which of the following is LEAST likely to occur as a result of a retrospective?
opts: The quality of future test objects improves by identifying improvements in development practices
Test efficiency improves by speeding up the configuration of test environments through automation
End users' understanding of the development and test processes is improved
Automated test scripts are enhanced through feedback from developers
exp: a) Is not correct. Retrospectives identify process improvements, which if implemented can improve the quality of future outputs — this is likely to occur. b) Is not correct. Retrospectives can result in increased test efficiency through process improvements — this is likely to occur. c) Is correct (LEAST likely). Retrospective participants typically include testers, developers, architects, product owners, and business analysts. End users are rarely invited and are unlikely to receive reports from these meetings, so their understanding of the development and test processes will not improve through retrospectives. d) Is not correct. A benefit of retrospectives includes improved testware quality through joint reviews with developers — this is likely to occur.

q: Which of the following test levels is MOST likely being performed if the testing is focused on validation and is not being performed by testers?
opts: Component testing
Component integration testing
System integration testing
Acceptance testing
exp: a) Is not correct. Component testing is mostly verification against a specification, not validation, and is typically performed by developers. b) Is not correct. Component integration testing is mostly verification against a specification, not validation, and is typically performed by developers. c) Is not correct. System integration testing is mostly verification against a specification and is typically performed by testers. d) Is correct. Acceptance testing focuses on validating that the system meets users' business needs and is ready for deployment. Ideally, this testing is carried out by the end users (not testers).

q: The navigation system software has been updated due to it suggesting routes that break traffic laws, such as driving the wrong way down one-way streets. Which of the following BEST describes the testing that will be performed?
opts: Only confirmation testing
Confirmation testing then regression testing
Only regression testing
Regression testing then confirmation testing
exp: a) Is not correct. After confirmation testing verifies the fix, regression testing is also needed to ensure no defects were introduced in unchanged areas. b) Is correct. Confirmation testing will check that the updates have resulted in a correct implementation, then regression testing will ensure no defects have been introduced or uncovered in unchanged areas. c) Is not correct. Regression testing alone is insufficient — confirmation testing is also needed to verify the fix itself. d) Is not correct. Confirmation testing must precede regression testing — you first confirm the fix is correct, then check for unintended side effects.

q: Which of the following BEST identifies example defects that could be found by static testing (rather than dynamic testing)? i. Two different parts of the design specification disagree  ii. A response time is too long  iii. A path in the code cannot be reached during execution  iv. A variable is declared but never subsequently used  v. The amount of memory needed is too high
opts: ii, v
iii, v
i, ii, iv
i, iii, iv
exp: i. Disagreement between two parts of the design specification — this is a specification defect (inconsistency) most easily found by static testing. ✓ ii. Response time too long — this is a performance defect that can only be detected by executing the program (dynamic testing). ✗ iii. A path in the code cannot be reached — this is a coding defect (unreachable code) most easily found by static testing (code review or static analysis). ✓ iv. A variable declared but never used — this is a coding defect most easily found by static testing. ✓ v. Too much memory used — this is a performance defect detectable only by executing the program (dynamic testing). ✗ Therefore, i, iii, and iv are best found by static testing.

q: Which of the following is a benefit of early and frequent stakeholder feedback?
opts: Changes to requirements are understood and implemented earlier
It ensures business stakeholders understand user requirements
It allows product owners to change their requirements as often as they want
End users are told which requirements will not be implemented prior to release
exp: a) Is correct. Obtaining stakeholder feedback early and often ensures that any changes in stakeholder requirements are understood and implemented sooner. b) Is not correct. The feedback is from stakeholders — providing feedback is unlikely to improve their own understanding of user requirements. c) Is not correct. While changes can be understood and implemented sooner, this does not mean unlimited changes are encouraged. d) Is not correct. The feedback is from stakeholders (not to them). Communicating to end users what will not be implemented is a separate concern, and ideally all needed requirements should be implemented.

q: Match review types (1-4) to descriptions (A-D): 1. Technical review  2. Informal review  3. Inspection  4. Walkthrough  /  A. Includes objectives such as gaining consensus, generating new ideas, and motivating authors to improve  B. Includes objectives such as educating reviewers, gaining consensus, generating new ideas and detecting potential defects  C. The main objective is detecting potential defects and it requires metrics collection to support process improvement  D. The main objective is detecting potential defects and it generates no formal documented output
exp: 1. Technical review — performed by technically qualified reviewers, objectives include gaining consensus, making decisions on technical problems, generating new ideas, and motivating authors to improve (A). 2. Informal review — main objective is detecting anomalies; the process is not defined and does not require formal documented output (D). 3. Inspection — most formal review type; primary objective is finding the most anomalies; requires metrics collection to support SDLC improvement (C). 4. Walkthrough — led by the author; objectives include educating reviewers, gaining consensus, generating new ideas, and detecting anomalies (B). Therefore: 1A, 2D, 3C, 4B.

q: Which of the following is a factor that contributes to a successful review?
opts: Ensure management participate as reviewers
Split large work products into smaller parts
Set reviewer evaluation as an objective
Plan to cover one document per review
exp: a) Is not correct. Management's support for the review process is important, but they should not necessarily participate as reviewers. b) Is correct. Breaking the work product into parts small enough to be reviewed in a reasonable timescale prevents reviewers from losing focus. c) Is not correct. Clear objectives and measurable exit criteria should be defined without evaluating the participants themselves. d) Is not correct. Large documents should be broken into smaller chunks — planning to cover an entire document in one review is the opposite of a success factor.

q: What is the MAIN difference between black-box test techniques and experience-based test techniques?
opts: The test object
The test level at which the test technique is used
The test basis
The software development lifecycle (SDLC) in which the test technique can be used
exp: a) Is not correct. Both types of techniques can generally be used for the same test objects. b) Is not correct. Both types can be used at all test levels. c) Is correct. Black-box test techniques are based on an analysis of the specified behavior of the test object (the test basis is usually a specification). Experience-based techniques use the knowledge and experience of testers — the tester may not use the specification at all when designing tests. d) Is not correct. Both types of techniques can be used in all SDLCs.

q: A PIN is valid if it consists of exactly four digits and not all the same digit. Valid partitions: (1) length correct = 4 digits, (2) length incorrect = not 4 digits, (3) number of different digits correct = at least 2 different digits, (4) number of different digits incorrect = all same digit. Which set BEST covers all identified equivalence partitions?
exp: a) Is correct. '12' covers 'length incorrect (too few digits)'. '1111' covers 'length correct' and 'number of different digits incorrect (all same)'. '1234' covers 'length correct' and 'number of different digits correct'. '12345' covers 'length incorrect (too many digits)'. All four partitions are covered. b) Is not correct. All partitions are covered, but only the lower side of 'length incorrect' is covered (no value with too many digits). c) Is not correct. There is no value covering a correct valid PIN ('length correct' + 'different digits correct'). d) Is not correct. There is no value covering 'number of different digits incorrect' (all same digit).

q: Business rule: IF (value ≤ 100 OR value ≥ 200) THEN 'value incorrect' ELSE 'value OK'. Using 2-value BVA, which set of test inputs achieves the greatest coverage?
exp: The equivalence partitions are: {…,99,100} (incorrect), {101,…,199} (OK), {200,201,…} (incorrect). There are 4 boundary values: 100, 101, 199, 200. In 2-value BVA, each boundary value and its closest neighbor in the adjacent partition form a coverage item. Since the closest neighbors are also boundary values, there are 4 coverage items: 100, 101, 199, 200. a) Covers only 100 and 200 as valid coverage items (2/4 = 50%). b) Covers only 100 and 200 as valid coverage items (50%). c) Covers only 100 and 101 as valid coverage items (50%). d) Is correct. Covers 101, 199, and 200 as valid coverage items (3/4 = 75%).

q: Decision table for driving license: R1 (C1=-, C2=T, C3=T → issue license), R2 (C1=-, C2=F, C3=- → additional lessons), R3 (C1=F, C2=-, C3=F → take exam again). What test data will show that there are contradictory rules?
opts: C1 = T, C2 = T, C3 = F
C1 = T, C2 = F, C3 = T
C1 = T, C2 = T, C3 = T and C1 = F, C2 = T, C3 = T
C1 = F, C2 = F, C3 = F
exp: a) Is not correct. (T, T, F) does not match any rule — this is an example of omission, not contradiction. b) Is not correct. (T, F, T) matches only R2, so there is no contradiction. c) Is not correct. Both (T, T, T) and (F, T, T) match only R1, so there is no contradiction. d) Is correct. (F, F, F) matches both R2 (C2=F condition) AND R3 (C1=F, C3=F conditions), but R2 calls for additional driving lessons while R3 calls for taking the exam again — two different actions for the same input combination, demonstrating a contradiction.

q: State transition diagram with states: START, REQUESTING, WAITING LIST, CONFIRMED, END. What is the MINIMUM number of test cases required to achieve 100% valid transitions coverage?
exp: Three transitions cannot appear in the same test case: 'REQUESTING→CONFIRMED', 'WAITING LIST→CONFIRMED', and 'WAITING LIST→END'. This means at least three test cases are required. All other transitions can be combined with one or more of these. The three minimum test cases are: TC1: START→REQUESTING (Available)→CONFIRMED (Pay)→END. TC2: START→REQUESTING (Not available)→WAITING LIST (Available)→CONFIRMED (Pay)→END. TC3: START→REQUESTING (Not available)→WAITING LIST (Cancel)→END. Three test cases cover all valid transitions.

q: You want to apply branch testing to code represented by a given control flow graph. How many coverage items do you need to test?
exp: In branch testing, the coverage items are branches, which are represented by the edges of a control flow graph. There are 8 edges in the control flow graph described. Therefore, 8 coverage items are needed.

q: How can white-box testing be useful in support of black-box testing?
opts: White-box coverage measures can help testers evaluate black-box tests in terms of the code coverage achieved by these black-box tests
White-box coverage analysis can help testers identify unreachable fragments of the source code
Branch testing subsumes black-box test techniques, so achieving full branch coverage guarantees achieving full coverage of any black-box technique
White-box test techniques can provide coverage items for black-box techniques
exp: a) Is correct. Performing only black-box testing does not provide a measure of actual code coverage. White-box coverage measures provide an objective measurement that allows additional tests to be generated to increase coverage and confidence in the code. b) Is not correct. This is true but has nothing to do with supporting black-box testing specifically. c) Is not correct. In general there are no such subsumption relationships between white-box and black-box techniques. d) Is not correct. White-box techniques derive coverage items from the test object structure; black-box techniques derive coverage items from the specification. They use different sources.

q: A tester uses the following list when performing testing: Correct input not accepted / Incorrect input accepted / Wrong output format / Division by zero. What test technique is MOST PROBABLY being used?
opts: Exploratory testing
Fault attack
Checklist-based testing
Boundary value analysis
exp: a) Is not correct. Exploratory testing uses test charters, not a list of possible defects/failures. b) Is correct. Fault attacks are a methodical approach to error guessing that requires the tester to create or acquire a list of possible errors, defects, and failures, then design tests to identify defects or cause failures. This list is exactly a list of possible failures. c) Is not correct. Checklist-based testing uses checklists of test conditions — this list is of possible failures, making fault attack the most probable technique. d) Is not correct. BVA is based on analysis of boundary values of equivalence partitions — not applicable here.

q: Which of the following BEST describes how using checklist-based testing can result in increased coverage?
opts: Checklist items can be defined at a sufficiently low level of detail, so the tester can implement and execute detailed test cases based on these items
Checklists can be automated, so each time an automated test execution covers the checklist items, it results in additional coverage
Each checklist item should be tested separately and independently, so the elements cover different areas of the software
Two testers designing and executing tests based on the same high-level checklist items will typically perform the testing in slightly different ways
exp: a) Is not correct. Although testers can create detailed test cases from checklist items, this does not explain how increased coverage results. b) Is not correct. Checklist items should not be automated, and even if they were, automated scripts execute the same way each time — not resulting in increased coverage. c) Is not correct. Testing items separately affects test execution order but does not impact the coverage achieved. d) Is correct. If checklists are high-level, variability in actual testing is likely to occur. Two testers following the same high-level checklist may use different test data, steps, etc. — one will likely cover areas not covered by the other, resulting in increased overall coverage.

q: Which of the following provides the BEST example of a scenario-oriented acceptance criterion?
opts: The application must allow users to delete their account and all associated data upon request
When a customer adds an item to their cart and proceeds to checkout, they should be prompted to log in or create an account if they haven't already done so
IF (contain(product(23).Name, cart.products())) THEN return FALSE
The website must comply with the ICT Accessibility 508 Standards and ensure that all content is accessible to users with disabilities
exp: a) Is not correct. This describes a rule/regulation the system must adhere to (right to be forgotten) — an example of a rule-oriented acceptance criterion. b) Is correct. This describes a specific scenario (customer adds to cart → proceeds to checkout → prompted to log in) that must be realized by the system — a scenario-oriented acceptance criterion. c) Is not correct. This resembles a line of code implementing a business rule. Acceptance criteria should be written in language that business representatives can understand. d) Is not correct. This describes a regulation the system must comply with — an example of a rule-oriented acceptance criterion.

q: User story: As a Regular or Special user, I want to use my electronic floor card to access specific floors. AC1: Regular users have access to floors 1-3. AC2: Floor 4 is only accessible to Special users. AC3: Special users have all the access rights of Regular users. Which test case MOST reasonably tests AC3?
opts: Check that a Regular user can access floors 1 and 3
Check that a Regular user cannot access floor 4
Check that a Special user can access floor 5
Check that a Special user can access floors 1, 2 and 3
exp: AC3 states that Special users have all the access rights of Regular users (i.e., floors 1-3). To test this, we need to test a Special user's access to the floors that Regular users can access. a) Is not correct. This tests a Regular user, not a Special user — it does not directly test AC3. b) Is not correct. This also tests a Regular user's restriction, not AC3. c) Is not correct. There is no floor 5 in the acceptance criteria. Test cases should not extend the scope of the user story. d) Is correct. Checking that a Special user can access floors 1, 2, and 3 directly validates that Special users have the access rights of Regular users (AC3).

q: Which of the following is NOT a purpose of a test plan?
opts: To define test data and expected results for component tests and component integration tests
To define as exit criteria from the component test level that '100% statement coverage and 100% branch coverage must be achieved'
To describe what fields the test progress report shall contain and what should be the form of this report
To explain why system integration testing will be excluded from testing, although the test strategy requires this test level
exp: a) Is correct (NOT a purpose). The test plan may include test data requirements as part of the test approach, but not the detailed test data for test cases. Test data is part of the test cases themselves, not the test plan. It is also usually impossible to define detailed test data at test plan creation time. b) Is not correct. Defining entry and exit criteria is a legitimate purpose of a test plan. c) Is not correct. Defining documentation templates and report formats is typical content of a test plan to facilitate standardized communication. d) Is not correct. One purpose of a test plan is to demonstrate adherence to the test strategy, or to explain why testing deviates from it.

q: Estimation model: E(n) = (3*A(n-1) + A(n-2)) / 4. From the graph: A(3)=8 person-days, A(4)=6 person-days. What is the estimated amount of work for iteration #5?
opts: 10.5 person-days
8.25 person-days
6.5 person-days
9.4 person-days
exp: From the graph: A(4) = 6 person-days and A(3) = 8 person-days. Applying the formula: E(5) = (3 × A(4) + A(3)) / 4 = (3 × 6 + 8) / 4 = (18 + 8) / 4 = 26 / 4 = 6.5 person-days.

q: Seven test cases TC1-TC7 with priorities (1=highest) and dependencies (arrows). TC4→TC5, TC4→TC7, TC1→TC2, TC2→TC5. Priorities: TC5(1), TC7(1), TC1(2), TC3(2), TC4(2), TC2(3), TC6(3). Which test case should be executed SIXTH?
opts: TC 3
TC 5
TC 6
TC 2
exp: Priority order (ignoring dependencies): TC5, TC7 (priority 1) → TC1, TC3, TC4 (priority 2) → TC2, TC6 (priority 3). However, dependencies must be respected: TC7 requires TC4 first. TC5 requires TC4 and TC2, but TC2 requires TC1 first. To run highest-priority tests as early as possible: TC4 must run first (enables TC7), then TC7 (priority 1), then TC1 (needed for TC2), then TC2 (needed for TC5), then TC5 (priority 1). After these five: TC3 has higher priority (2) than TC6 (3), so TC3 runs sixth. Full schedule: TC4 → TC7 → TC1 → TC2 → TC5 → TC3 → TC6.

q: What does the test pyramid model show?
opts: That tests may have different priorities
That tests may have different granularity
That tests may require different coverage criteria
That tests may depend on other tests
exp: a) Is not correct. The test pyramid model does not provide information about test priorities. b) Is correct. The test pyramid model shows that different tests have different levels of granularity — from fine-grained unit/component tests at the base to coarser system and acceptance tests at the top. c) Is not correct. The test pyramid model is independent of coverage criteria. d) Is not correct. The test pyramid model does not show dependencies between tests.

q: What is the relationship between the testing quadrants, test levels and test types?
opts: Testing quadrants represent particular combinations of test levels and test types, defining their location in the software development lifecycle
Testing quadrants describe the degree of granularity of individual test types performed at each test level
Testing quadrants assign the test types that can be performed to the test levels
Testing quadrants group test levels and test types by several criteria such as targeting specific stakeholders
exp: a) Is not correct. Testing quadrants group test levels and test types separately — they do not represent specific combinations or define SDLC locations. b) Is not correct. The model describing granularity of test levels is the test pyramid, not testing quadrants. c) Is not correct. In general, any test type can be performed at any test level. d) Is correct. The testing quadrants group test levels, test types, test activities, test techniques, and work products by several criteria. Tests can be business-facing or technology-facing, and can support the team (guide development) or critique the product (measure behavior against expectations).

q: Which of the following is an example of how product risk analysis may influence the thoroughness and scope of testing?
opts: Continuous risk monitoring allows us to identify an emerging risk as soon as possible
Risk identification allows us to implement risk mitigation activities and reduce the risk level
The assessed risk level helps us to select the rigor of testing
Risk analysis allows us to derive coverage items
exp: a) Is not correct. Continuous risk monitoring is part of risk control, not risk analysis. b) Is not correct. Mitigating actions are defined during the risk control phase, not directly from risk identification. c) Is correct. The assessed risk level directly influences the thoroughness and scope of testing — higher risk areas receive more rigorous testing. d) Is not correct. Coverage items are derived using test techniques, not through risk analysis.

q: Which of the following activities in the test process makes the MOST use of test progress reports?
opts: Test design
Test completion
Test analysis
Test planning
exp: a) Is not correct. Test progress reports are not primarily used during test design. b) Is correct. A test completion report is prepared during test completion and uses information from test progress reports and other data. Test completion makes the most use of test progress reports. c) Is not correct. Test progress reports are not primarily used during test analysis. d) Is not correct. Test progress reports are most used during test monitoring/control and test completion, not test planning.

q: Which of the following is NOT an example of how configuration management supports testing?
opts: All commits to the repository are uniquely identified and version controlled
All changes in the test environment elements are tracked
All requirement specifications are referenced unambiguously in test plans
All identified defects have an assigned status
exp: a) Is not correct. Unique identification and version control of commits allows reassembling the software version a user experienced, helping reproduce failures — this is a CM support for testing. b) Is not correct. Tracking changes to test environment elements allows testers to roll back to a previous version if changes cause unexpected issues — this is a CM support for testing. c) Is not correct. CM ensures all documentation is referenced unambiguously in test documentation (e.g., test plans) — this is a CM support for testing. d) Is correct (NOT CM). Assigning a status to defects is ensured by defect management, not configuration management.

q: Defect report for WebShop v0.99: 'Login button not working. Steps: Launch website, click login button. Expected: redirected to login page. Actual: button does not respond. Severity: High. Priority: Urgent.' What is the MOST important missing information?
opts: Name of the tester and date
Test environment elements and their version numbers
Identification of the test object
Impact on the interests of stakeholders
exp: a) Is not correct. The tester's name and date are important but not as critical as test environment information for reproducing the defect. b) Is correct. The most important missing information is the browser and device used for testing. A login button may work fine on one browser/version but not on another. Browser and device information helps developers reproduce the issue and find the root cause more quickly. c) Is not correct. The test object is identified (WebShop v0.99). d) Is not correct. The impact/severity is included (Severity: High).

q: Tools from which of the following categories help with the organization of test cases, detected defects and configuration management?
opts: Test execution and coverage tools
Test design and implementation tools
Defect management tools
Test management tools
exp: a) Is not correct. Test execution and coverage tools facilitate automated test execution and coverage measurement — they do not help with organizing defects and configuration management. b) Is not correct. Test design and implementation tools facilitate test case and test data generation — they do not help with organizing defects and configuration management. c) Is not correct. Defect management tools manage defects but are not testing tools and are not used to organize test cases or configuration management. d) Is correct. Test management tools increase test process efficiency by facilitating management of the SDLC, requirements, tests, defects, and configuration management.

q: Which of the following is MOST likely to be a benefit of test automation?
opts: The capability of generating test cases without access to the test basis
The achievement of increased coverage through more objective assessment
The increase in test execution times available with higher processing power
The prevention of human errors through greater consistency and repeatability
exp: a) Is not correct. Generating test cases always requires access to the test basis — neither testers nor tools can generate test cases without it (expected results must come from the specification). b) Is not correct. While test automation provides more objective coverage assessment, that objective measurement alone does not increase coverage — additional test cases must be written based on the results. c) Is not correct. This is a contradictory statement — higher processing power would normally reduce (not increase) execution times, and increased execution times are not a benefit. d) Is correct. Test automation prevents human errors through greater consistency and repeatability — tests are consistently derived from requirements, data is created systematically, and tests execute in the same order with the same frequency.

q: Which of the following statements describe a valid test objective?
opts: To prove that there are no unfixed defects in the system under test
To prove that there will be no failures after the implementation of the system into production
To reduce the risk level of the test object and to build confidence in the quality level
To verify that there are no untested combinations of inputs
exp: a) Is not correct. It is impossible to prove that there are no defects anymore in the system under test (see testing principle 1). b) Is not correct. See testing principle 7. c) Is correct. Testing finds defects and failures which reduces the level of risk and at the same time gives more confidence in the quality level of the test object. d) Is not correct. It is impossible to test all combinations of inputs (see testing principle 2).

q: Which of the following options shows an example of test activities that contribute to success?
opts: Having testers involved during various software development lifecycle (SDLC) activities will help to detect defects in work products
Testers try not to disturb the developers while coding, so that the developers write better code
Testers collaborating with end users help to improve the quality of defect reports during component integration and system testing
Certified testers will design much better test cases than non-certified testers
exp: a) Is correct. It is important that testers are involved from the beginning of the SDLC. It will increase understanding of design decisions and will detect defects early. b) Is not correct. Both developers and testers will have more understanding of each other's work products. c) Is not correct. End users will not help testers in increasing the quality of defect reports; also, users usually do not participate in low-level testing. d) Is not correct. Being certified does not automatically mean that the tester will be better in test design.

q: You have been assigned as a tester to a team producing a new system incrementally.
